Stopping CSRF in ThinkPHP with Form Tokens

Now that we're done with form validation, this post is all about form Tokens. The thing is, a form Token only works when it's paired with the validation we covered earlier, which is exactly why I left it until after the validation chapter. So let's get into what a form Token actually is.
What a form Token is for
First off, a form Token mainly exists to deal with CSRF. CSRF stands for
(article in Mandarin) in one of my earlier posts, so I'll just pull in part of that here.
In short, CSRF abuses the trust your browser has in a website to pull off an attack. Say we already know some API path, like the delete-account API
. A hacker then sends you a message: "Quick, click this link and you'll get a free Bitcoin!" (yeah, right)
You don't think twice and just click it. If the site hasn't set up any CSRF protection, all the site sees is "Oh, you've got my Cookie and Session, so you want to delete the account with

? Sure thing." And just like that, the site happily deletes the first account. To stop this from happening, you need to add CSRF protection on the site: when data gets sent to the backend, it has to carry along a random string (kind of like a hash) for verification, and only if that checks out does the request go through. But this string isn't something you can just make up and attach yourself — it has to be generated by the backend and handed to the frontend after the browser triggers it.
If you want a deeper dive into CSRF, check out
Normally, if you're writing Express.js, you have to install an extra package — namely
. But ThinkPHP ships with its own CSRF solution built in. The catch is that it has to be paired with form validation, which is why I'm only introducing form Tokens now.

And just like that, we've successfully defended against CSRF. But you might be wondering whether you can customize that error message — and you can. Same deal: open
and add a custom message under

protected $message = [
    'email.require' => 'Email 必填',
    'email.email'=> 'Email 格式錯誤',
    'email.token' => 'Token 出現問題請重新整理。', // Token error message
    'password.require' => '密碼欄位必填',
    'password.min' => '密碼最短長度為 8',
    'password.max' => '密碼長度最長為 30',
    'gender.require' => '性別必填',
    'gender' => '性別必須為是數字'
];
Hit it one more time and you'll see the message has changed ↓
Token 出現問題請重新整理。

That pretty much covers the validation side of things. If you want to dig deeper into validation and how to build your own custom validators, I'd recommend the validation chapter in the official manual — it goes into a lot more detail →

Now that form validation is out of the way, it's time to talk about form Tokens. A form Token only works alongside the validation we set up earlier, which is exactly why I saved it for after the validation chapter. Let's dig into what a form Token actually is and how it stops CSRF attacks in ThinkPHP.
